tweets.text:
You buy a $2,000,000 home.

You put a 20% down payment.

You take out a $1.6M 30yr fixed mortgage.

Let’s exclude property tax and insurance for a second.

@ 3.0% your monthly payment = $6,746

@ 6.65%, your monthly payment = $10,271

6.65% is today’s current rates and there’s signs of it going higher…

3.0% was right around 2021-2022 rates.

Will we ever see those again?

tweets.text:
I am expecting API to show a build in crude storage this week. 

We have -0.4 million bbls for commercial crude. API overestimated last week’s crude draw, so API would, in theory, show a build of 1-2 million bbls. 

If it’s below that, EIA will show a larger draw in crude. 

If it’s above that, EIA will show a build in crude.

tweets.text:
#Bitcoin / $BTC

This question challenged me to think critically about what else is a "bear case".

Outside of what I listed, the biggest one left on the table is cyclical performance.

The last 3 bull market peaks occurred ~536 days post their respective BTC halvings. All within 13 days of that number.

On the same note, the last 2 bear market bottoms occurred ~908 days post halving, both numbers within a few weeks of that number.

Which brings us to where we are now, ~769 days post halving... it's a small data set to go off of, and there are plenty of other external factors that affect each cycle differently - but I still would see it as a decent counter-argument to any *short term* bullish bias I have. We're still ~4.5 months out from what would be the bottom if we were to continue to follow what's been a consistent trend (e.g. bottom in October 2026).

Fwiw - if you look at the four month average price of BTC on either side of those bottoms, they were all incredible opportunities to stack more Bitcoin.

So like I also said in the interview - now isn't when you want to sit back and trade leverage because you're bored.

Instead, leverage your boredom and spend that time to build something great.

I'd love to see a bounce at $70K like I've shared over the last several weeks - but I'm fully prepared to use any extra time the market gives me to keep working harder and stack more.

Be more bullish.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Kaleo's bear case, in order of what he's actually worried about:

1. Equities top. Everything else follows from this. Bitcoin is weak enough that it can't absorb a macro shock independently.

2. Trump. Not a specific prediction. Just the acknowledgment that the distribution of https://t.co/pA5qkkehZg
tweets.full_text:
#Bitcoin / $BTC

This question challenged me to think critically about what else is a "bear case".

Outside of what I listed, the biggest one left on the table is cyclical performance.

The last 3 bull market peaks occurred ~536 days post their respective BTC halvings. All within 13 days of that number.

On the same note, the last 2 bear market bottoms occurred ~908 days post halving, both numbers within a few weeks of that number.

Which brings us to where we are now, ~769 days post halving... it's a small data set to go off of, and there are plenty of other external factors that affect each cycle differently - but I still would see it as a decent counter-argument to any *short term* bullish bias I have. We're still ~4.5 months out from what would be the bottom if we were to continue to follow what's been a consistent trend (e.g. bottom in October 2026).

Fwiw - if you look at the four month average price of BTC on either side of those bottoms, they were all incredible opportunities to stack more Bitcoin.

So like I also said in the interview - now isn't when you want to sit back and trade leverage because you're bored.

Instead, leverage your boredom and spend that time to build something great.

I'd love to see a bounce at $70K like I've shared over the last several weeks - but I'm fully prepared to use any extra time the market gives me to keep working harder and stack more.

Be more bullish.

thread_tweets.text:
@KarelCrypteau A decrease of OI during a pump probably means that shorts are getting rekt which is good (assuming you're a bull of course).

However, if the pump turns into a rally and there's no increase in OI at all that's more often than not a sign that there's no sustained demand.
thread_tweets.full_text:
@KarelCrypteau A decrease of OI during a pump probably means that shorts are getting rekt which is good (assuming you're a bull of course).

However, if the pump turns into a rally and there's no increase in OI at all that's more often than not a sign that there's no sustained demand.

tweets.text:
couple UX issues on this wallet imo:
- the initial node sync is very slow (some wallets have a sync from x block height that would help)
- switching transparent/shielded remains confusing
- I've had transactions fail and needed to change servers (us to jp for example)

best privacy is just withdraw from CEX to fresh wallet on chain of choice
